chunk_summary: This section discusses anthropometry and growth interpretation in children with neurodisability, emphasizing alternative measurement methods, body composition proxies, and growth chart usage tailored to their unique needs.
learning_objectives: Understand the limitations of traditional weight and height measurements in children with neurodisability.
Learn alternative methods for estimating stature, such as knee height and ulna length, and their clinical application.
Explore the use of mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) and triceps skinfold as proxies for body composition.
Interpret growth charts and identify malnutrition risks in children with severe cerebral palsy.
key_facts: Contractures, scoliosis, and altered body composition can misrepresent nutritional risk in children with neurodisability.
Segmental measures like knee height, tibial length, and ulna length can estimate stature using validated equations.
Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) and triceps skinfold are useful proxies for tracking fat and muscle stores.
WHO growth charts are appropriate up to 5 years when reliable height data is available; CP-specific charts can contextualize growth trajectories.
Persistent low weight or falling growth velocity should trigger active nutritional intervention regardless of the growth chart used.
reference_quote: Nutritional assessment in children with neurologic impairment should not rely on 'weight and height only,' because contractures, scoliosis, and altered body composition can misrepresent nutritional risk. (ESPGHAN Advice Guide)
document_key_topics: Alternative anthropometric methods for children with neurodisability
Body composition proxies for nutritional assessment
Growth chart interpretation in cerebral palsy
